params.text:
Common routes and forms of medicines

Medicines come in many forms and are given by different routes — the way the medicine enters the body. Each has its own handling and recording requirements, and giving a medicine by the wrong route can be dangerous.

  • Oral — tablets, capsules and liquids taken by mouth and swallowed.
  • Topical — creams, ointments and gels applied to the skin.
  • Inhaled — inhalers and nebulisers breathed into the lungs.
  • Drops — eye, ear or nose drops.
  • Patches (transdermal) — medicine absorbed slowly through the skin. Record where the patch is placed and rotate the site to avoid skin damage.

Some medicines look similar but work very differently, so always check the label and MAR chart for the correct form and route.

params.question: It is safe to crush a tablet to make it easier to swallow, without checking with the pharmacist first.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. Crushing a tablet can be dangerous — it may change how the medicine works or alter the dose, and it is often outside the licence. Always check with the pharmacist or prescriber first.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Crushing can change how a medicine is absorbed and may be unsafe. Never crush or alter a medicine without advice from the pharmacist or prescriber.
